exemplar.title: Investigation of the relationship between liquid level and the frequency produced by a singing champagne glass

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigation of the relationship between liquid level and the frequency produced by a singing champagne glass

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Tables and graphs are both clear and precise: consistent significant figures, labelled axes, units and error bars
Thorough uncertainty treatment: instrument errors stated, random spread from repeats, propagation with fractional methods, intercept uncertainty determined
Effective data processing: means, log transformation, and fitting executed appropriately with only trivial transcription slips
weaknesses: Ambient noise level not quantified (e.g. in dB) to support qualitative controls
Error propagation formulae appear without source citation
Minor notation inconsistencies in table headers
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: With clarity and precision in data recording (band 6), largely appropriate uncertainty consideration (band 5) and mostly accurate processing (band 5), the work satisfies all top-band criteria. The minor omissions do not compromise the overall excellence, justifying the full 6 marks.

strengths: Focused research question embedded in A. P. Frenchâs model with explicit manipulated and measured variables
Comprehensive methodology: ten 22 mL increments, five repeats, video playback, uncertainty quantification and control of temperature, noise, etc.
Concise theoretical background linking champagne glass resonance to the ideal cylinder model with defined parameters
weaknesses: Missing specific apparatus details (glass make/model, smartphone app name) hindering full reproducibility
Did not discuss assumptions or limitations of the ideal cylinder approximation
Procedure omits time-interval control for temperature drift
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands reach or exceed the top band descriptors: a specific contextualized research question (band 6), a reproducible methodology with only minor omissions (band 5), and well-reasoned methodological considerations (band 4). Overall performance is at the uppermost level, meriting the maximum 6 marks.

strengths: Conclusion directly justified by quantitative results (intercept) and consistent with analysis
Relevant comparison made between experimental intercept and theoretical expectation, situating findings in accepted scientific context
weaknesses: No numerical comparison of the experimental gradient to the theoretical slope
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is fully supported by the data and makes a justified comparison to the accepted scientific model. Both strands meet the top-band descriptors, awarding the maximum 6 marks.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses identified (phone precision, finger pressure, reading error)
Realistic improvements proposed (professional equipment, mechanical rubbing, automated readings)
weaknesses: Impact of each weakness on overall uncertainty is not quantified
Improvements are described but not explained in terms of their effect on precision or accuracy
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation goes beyond generic statements by naming concrete limitations and suggesting relevant improvements (band 3). However, it lacks an explanation of each improvementâs expected impact, placing it in the lower half of the 3â4 band and meriting 3 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a focused research question that explicitly identifies both the manipulated variable (distance from water surface to rim) and the measured response (resonance frequency), and immediately embeds it within the context of A. P. Frenchâs theoretical model for singing wine-glasses.  A concise but specific theoretical background, complete with the key governing equation and clearly defined parameters, demonstrates why this question is scientifically interesting and how it links to prior literature.  Because the context is not simply âsoundâ in general but the resonance of a champagne glass as an approximate cylinder, the description is judged specific and appropriate, fully satisfying the top-band descriptor.

Instrument uncertainties are stated; random spread is captured through five repeats and propagated via fractional methods; error bars are displayed and an uncertainty on the intercept is calculated with max/min gradients.  Systematic effects of phone calibration or liquid density are mentioned only qualitatively; nevertheless, the treatment is largely appropriate with minor omissions, fitting the lower end of the top band.

Methodological considerations are more than merely listed: the student gives reasons for each control variable and chooses a broad data range (empty to nearly full) with five repeats, producing 55 readings.  However, the justification of sample size, increment size, and the adequacy of smartphone precision is only described, not rigorously explained or supported by calculations of expected resolution.  Thus the work aligns with the âdescribedâ level of sufficiency rather than a fully âexplainedâ evaluation, meriting the upper part of the 3-4 band.

The evaluation table lists three weaknesses (phone precision, finger pressure, reading error) but treats them generically and does not articulate their relative impact on the final uncertainty or validity.  This matches the descriptor âstates generic methodological weaknessesâ, placing the strand in band 1-2; both weaknesses identified are indeed realistic, so the higher mark of 2 is justified.

The procedure states ten equal 22 mL additions, five repeat readings at every level, use of video playback to capture extrema, and identification of all measuring devices.  Control of temperature, phone position, background noise and finger moisture is explained, and uncertainties for ruler (Â±0.1 cm) and app (Â±0.01 Hz) are specified.  Although the exact make of the glass and the phone model are omitted, the level of detail is still sufficient for another student to reproduce the work with only minor ambiguities, hence top-band is appropriate; a score of 5 (rather than 6) recognises the residual gaps.

Means, fractional uncertainty propagation and logarithmic transformation to test linearity are executed correctly; graphs correspond to the processed values.  A few transcription slips (e.g. f_a labels) do not materially affect accuracy.  Processing is therefore appropriate and largely accurate, warranting a score of 5 in the highest band.

Improvements such as using professional equipment, mechanical rubbing, automated readings and calipers are realistic, but they are merely stated with minimal elaboration.  The link between each improvement and its expected effect on data quality is not explained, aligning with ârealistic improvements are statedâ (band 1-2) at the upper score.

Data tables include headings, units and uncertainties; significant figures are internally consistent; processed tables and both non-linear and linear graphs carry clearly labelled axes, scales and error bars.  Narrative references (e.g. Figure numbers) further enhance clarity.  Both clarity and precision are therefore evident at the highest standard.

The student compares the experimental intercept with the theoretical expectation of 1 and comments that the model is âa reasonable approximationâ.  While this constitutes a relevant comparison, it remains limited to the intercept and does not analyse the gradient or possible departures at larger values, so the work fits the âsome relevant comparisonâ descriptor (band 3-4) at the upper level.

The student states the observed trend and directly links it to the quantitative result (intercept = 0.94 Â± 0.01) extracted from the linear graph, confirming that decreasing water level lowers the resonance frequency.  The conclusion is consistent with and justified by the data; however, numerical comparison with theoretical slope is absent, so a perfect (6) is not awarded.

annotations.comment: The introductionâs personal narrative, while engaging, contains general reflections on music rather than situating the research question in a specific scientific context. The student should reframe to emphasize the relevance of singing glasses within acoustics research.
Control variables table is thorough, yet the make and model of the smartphone app are not specified, which hinders reproducibility; include precise apparatus details.
Inclusion of the reference supports the temperature control rationale; well aligned with methodological considerations.
Justification for using a cylindrical-like glass is well reasoned; this is a strength in linking apparatus choice to theoretical model.
The aim is clearly stated, but the research question (how frequency changes with water volume) is introduced late; consider articulating a precise research question upfront to improve clarity.

The theoretical background explains sound propagation well, but lacks direct connection to the wineglass resonance equation; integrate references to equation (1) earlier to strengthen conceptual linkage.
This paragraph thoroughly describes longitudinal versus transverse waves, a strength in demonstrating understanding of basic wave mechanics.
Comprehensive explanation of the resonance mechanism demonstrates depth of understanding; consider condensing to focus on information directly relevant to your experiment.
Equation (1) is presented without specifying assumptions or limitations; the student should discuss the ideal cylinder assumption and its applicability to a champagne glass.
The explanation of Î² is clear, but the notation change from Î± to Î² may confuse readers; justify this change explicitly in the text.
The research question and hypothesis are defined, but the hypothesis uses Î¼^* instead of H^* (inconsistent notation); correct this to maintain clarity.
The bibliography reference number superscripts are mismatched; ensure each citation in the text corresponds to the correct footnote.
Listing kitchen scales explains volume control, but you should specify the manufacturer and least count for reproducibility.
The procedure is systematic but would benefit from specifying the time interval between measurements to account for cooling or temperature drift.
Using mass to approximate volume is pragmatic, but convert mass to volume explicitly using density, rather than assuming direct ml equivalence.
Good consideration of data point selection but the justification refers to a qualitative âfull spectrumâ; discuss sample spacing rationale quantitatively.
The method of recording screen and averaging min/max is inventive, yet this approach may introduce selection bias; apply statistical measures (e.g., standard deviation) to gauge spread.
Uncertainties are stated concisely, but fractional propagation formulas require referencing a source; include a citation to the error propagation method.
The effort to minimize background noise is detailed, but lacks quantified noise level measurement (e.g., dB); consider reporting ambient noise levels.
Clear rationale for using distilled water highlights control of density variables; good methodological justification.
Equation (2) is correct, but the notation Îf should be clarified as half-range uncertainty explicitly in the text.
The raw data table is well formatted with uncertainties, but the Â± notation on the header row appears inconsistent; align header uncertainties with column data.
Introducing the raw data concisely is effective; consider summarizing emerging trends in the text to guide the reader before presenting the full table.
Caption provides a figure number, but should clarify that this is a data table (Table 2) rather than Figure 2 for correctness.
Clear outline of propagation steps reflects strong application of error analysis methods from the IB data booklet.
The intermediate uncertainty propagation steps are thorough, but the final expression could be simplified to reduce nested fractions for readability.
Rationale for decimal precision is justified, but a discussion of potential rounding impacts on the smallest values would enhance rigor.
Conclusion reiterates trend and links to intercept, but lacks comparison of experimental slope to theoretical value; include slope analysis to fully justify conclusions.
The evaluation lists specific weaknesses but does not assess their relative impact on the data trends; quantify how each limitation affects your results to deepen reflection.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â

similarExemplars.title:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

userSubmission.researchQuestion:
To what extent does decreasing the volume of air in a champagne bottle by adding water inside
affect the resonant frequency that is produced when the glass is set into an air-induced vibration
by blowing air across the opening of the neck?

similarExemplars.title:
How does the length of the pipe affect the natural frequency of the air column in the pipe at constant air temperature when the pipe is:
a)	open at both ends and air is blown close to one end?
b)	open at one and closed at the other end, and air is blown close to the open end?
Natural frequency is the frequency that has the highest intensity and is the loudest of all frequencies which are produced with air blowing.
